read.title: Useful Idiot, Predictive History, And The Facts Trap
read.subtitle: A cleaned reading of the Zeteo interview
read.voice_policy: Direct argumentative prose derived from the interview. Preserve interviewer pressure where it changes the meaning of Jiang's answers. Do not impersonate first-person authorship except when quoting verified source material.
read.trace_policy: Paragraph-level transcript refs. Speculative claims remain marked as Jiang's speculative analysis and dated predictions remain tied to the 2026-04-03 source.
read.dek: Mehdi Hasan does not let Jiang enjoy the Nostradamus frame. He tests the method, the misses, the China question, the Professor persona, the facts-versus-truth distinction, and the language around Jews, Israel, and empire.
read.thesis: The interview is valuable because it puts Jiang's prediction machine under hostile conditions. He can explain Iran through zero-sum strategy, asymmetric leverage, and the occupation trap. He can also admit that the Nikki Haley part of the prediction was wrong, that his virality may not be organic, and that his method is speculative rather than fact-proof in the narrow journalistic sense. Hasan's pressure exposes the tension at the center of this source: Jiang wants truth deep enough to predict history, while the interviewer keeps asking what happens when imagination outruns evidence, credentials, and dangerous language.

opening.heading: Core Reading
opening.text: The cold open gives the whole interview away. Jiang is introduced as China's Nostradamus, then immediately pushed through the weak points: Haley was wrong, facts matter, China may be amplifying him, and he calls himself Professor Jiang though he is not a professor. The useful read is not that Jiang discusses Iran. The useful read is that Hasan turns Iran into a stress test for Jiang's entire epistemology: what counts as prediction, what counts as evidence, and who benefits when the prediction goes viral.

beats.heading: Left, Far Right, And 2026
beats.summary: Jiang names his politics, accepts that many would now call him far right, and closes with three dark 2026 war predictions.

paragraphs.text: Asked for his politics, Jiang says he has always been on the left: redistribution, free speech, antiwar, anti-empire, individual autonomy. Then comes the inversion. Given those preferences, he says, most people would now consider him far right. The line is a self-diagnosis of the interview itself: the labels no longer settle the argument.
